ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Lao Beijing Ma Doufu (fermented mung bean paste residue). FOODON_03311737 (mung bean food product) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for fermented mung bean paste (Ma Doufu). A00LC (Legume-based products) is the closest defensible parent. A more specific code for fermented legume paste does not appear in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Lao Beijing Ma Doufu or fermented mung bean paste residue. SNOMED CT does not catalogue this traditional Beijing preparation.

main_ingredients: mung bean residue (byproduct of mung bean starch or Dou Zhi production)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Lao Beijing Ma Doufu is approximately 200 g, served as a shared side dish or snack alongside other Beijing street foods.

nutrient_source.title: Mung beans, mature seeds, cooked, boiled, without salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174256); adapted for fermented mung bean paste with stir-frying and added condiments
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct nutrient entry for Lao Beijing Ma Doufu (fermented mung bean paste residue) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked mung beans (FDC ID 174256) as the closest defensible base ingredient, with upward adjustment to sodium_mg (~350 mg/100 g) to reflect added fermented black beans (douchi), salt, and condiments typical of the Beijing preparation. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate are modestly adjusted to reflect the stir-frying step and added oil (~5 g oil per 100 g finished dish). This is an estimated composition; a direct laboratory analysis of the restaurant dish would be required for high-confidence values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; mung beans, cooked (GI = 31, glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lao Beijing Ma Doufu (fermented mung bean paste residue). GI of 31 is taken from cooked mung beans as published in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which is sourced in the University of Sydney GI Database. Fermentation and stir-frying are not expected to substantially raise the GI above the mung bean baseline; if anything, fermentation may lower the effective GI. GL per serving calculated as: (31 × 11.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 200 g serving) / 100 = 7.13, rounded to 7.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fermented black beans (douchi) and added salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 200 g serving of Lao Beijing Ma Doufu contributes approximately 700 mg sodium, representing roughly 30–35% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 31) and moderate dietary fibre from mung bean residue
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lao Beijing Ma Doufu has a low glycemic index of approximately 31 and provides around 3.8 g dietary fibre per 100 g, supporting gradual glucose absorption; a standard 200 g serving yields a glycemic load of approximately 7, which is within the low-GL range and suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as part of a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Plant-based protein, low saturated fat, and cholesterol-free profile from mung bean base
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is cholesterol-free and low in saturated fat (~0.5 g/100 g), with plant-based protein from mung beans supporting a heart-healthy dietary pattern; however, the moderate sodium content warrants attention for individuals with cardiovascular disease who are following sodium-restricted diets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lao Beijing Ma Doufu (老北京麻豆腐, literally 'Old Beijing hemp/mashed tofu') is a traditional Beijing street food and home-style dish made from the fibrous residue left over after extracting mung bean starch or producing Dou Zhi (mung bean sour milk). The residue is stir-fried in vegetable oil with fermented black beans (douchi), dried chili peppers, green onions, and ginger, producing a thick, slightly sour, savoury paste with a soft, grainy texture. The dish has a distinctive sour-salty flavour profile derived from the natural fermentation of the mung bean residue and the umami depth of douchi. It is considered a quintessential Beijing comfort food, closely associated with the hutong (alleyway) food culture of old Beijing, and is often paired with Dou Zhi as a complementary snack. Nutritionally, Ma Doufu is a moderate-energy, plant-based dish providing approximately 98 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein (~5.8 g), dietary fibre (~3.8 g), and a low glycemic index (~31) derived from its mung bean base. The sodium content is elevated due to fermented condiments (~350 mg/100 g), which is clinically relevant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and dairy-free. It is not commonly found outside Beijing and northern China, making it a culturally distinctive item in Chinese cuisine.

meal_context: paired with Dou Zhi (mung bean sour milk)

confidence_reason: No direct nutrient entry for Lao Beijing Ma Doufu exists in any approved primary or secondary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, USDA FoodData Central). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked mung beans with adjustments for stir-frying and added condiments. GI is estimated from the analogous cooked mung bean GI (31) per the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked mung beans (FDC ID 174256) adapted for the stir-fried, fermented preparation; a direct laboratory analysis of the finished dish is required for high-confidence nutrient data. 2. Sodium_mg is upwardly adjusted to approximately 350 mg/100 g to reflect fermented black beans (douchi) and added salt; this is an estimate. 3. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this preparation in the adapted source. 4. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported for plant-based mung bean preparations in the source database. 5. Vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted USDA source for this preparation. 6. Selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. 7. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based dish prepared with vegetable oil; naturally zero by ingredient composition. 8. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based, animal-product-free dish. 9. Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 10. GI of 31 is an analogous estimate from cooked mung beans (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Ma Doufu exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 11. The input description contained typographical errors ('Sour and Solty', 'Boons and Chil Includod') which have been corrected and replaced with accurate culinary and nutritional information. 12. The food is served at See Ya Tonight 今晚有局 (Singapore) at SGD 23.40; this is a restaurant-specific price and does not affect nutrient values. 13. This is a Chinese cuisine dish (Beijing origin), not a Singaporean dish; therefore Malay, Tamil, and Hindi multilingual names are not mandatory per G6 rules.
